title: Applications of additive combinatorics to Diophantine equations
speaker: Alexei Skorobogatov
abstract: The work of Green, Tao and Ziegler can be used to prove existence and approximation properties for rational solutions of the Diophantine equations that describe representations of a product of norm forms by a product of linear polynomials. One can also prove that the Brauer-Manin obstruction precisely describes the closure of rational points in the adelic points for pencils of conics and quadrics over \(\mathbb Q\) when the degenerate fibres are all defined over \(\mathbb Q\).

proof_stages.label: Conics and the conic-bundle surface
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Legendre’s local-global and weak-approximation theorem for conics, then sets up a one-parameter conic bundle surface over P1 and asks whether its rational points are dense or even Zariski dense beyond the obvious degenerate-fibre points.

proof_stages.label: Explicit Brauer--Manin obstruction and main density theorem
proof_stages.summary: Quaternion Brauer classes attached to the factors t-e_i are constructed, their Hilbert-symbol conditions define the relevant Brauer--Manin subset of adelic points, and the main theorem of Browning--Matthiesen--Skorobogatov is stated: X(Q) is dense in this Brauer--Manin set, implying weak weak approximation and Zariski density.

proof_stages.label: Descent to an auxiliary norm variety W
proof_stages.summary: Quadratic duality converts the Brauer--Manin conditions into constants c_i and local norm equations, producing a lift to an auxiliary variety W; the fibre product W ×_{P1} X is birational to W times a conic, so Legendre’s theorem reduces the arithmetic of X to the Hasse principle and weak approximation for W.

proof_stages.label: Green--Tao--Ziegler input for linear forms
proof_stages.summary: The needed additive-combinatorial theorem is recalled as the finite-complexity generalized Hardy--Littlewood theorem for systems of linear forms, together with a corollary producing pairs λ,μ that approximate prescribed local data while making each λ-e_i μ prime up to S-units.

proof_stages.label: Hasse principle and weak approximation for W
proof_stages.summary: Applying the corollary to local points on W, continuity handles the finitely many bad primes, the S-unit/almost-prime support makes all but one Hilbert symbol trivial, and global reciprocity forces the remaining local norm condition, giving a rational solution and hence the desired approximation theorem.

proof_stages.label: Torus-fibration generalization
proof_stages.summary: The same descent-plus-additive-combinatorics strategy is generalized to smooth proper varieties over P1 whose generic fibre is a principal homogeneous space under a Q-torus and whose bad fibres lie over rational points, again yielding equality between the adelic closure of rational points and the Brauer--Manin set.

proof_stages.label: Comparison with the fibration method
proof_stages.summary: A different fibration method is described as an alternative route to similar results, especially for families where descent does not separate variables into a torus product, and Schinzel’s hypothesis H is contrasted with the unconditional Green--Tao--Ziegler replacement in the linear split case over Q.

proof_stages.label: Higher-genus-base obstruction and zero-cycle outlook
proof_stages.summary: A counterexample over a base curve of genus at least one with a unique rational point shows that such rational-point results cannot extend naively beyond P1, since rational points are absent while Brauer--Manin and finite-descent obstructions vanish; the speaker closes by pointing to zero-cycles of degree one as the more plausible general framework.

examples.name: Small-number-of-degenerate-fibres case
examples.note: In response to an audience remark, the speaker notes that the case with very small complexity, such as n=3, was accessible by earlier methods, illustrating why the Green--Tao--Ziegler input is needed for the general theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: the small-number-of-degenerate-fibres case is only cited as a previously handled/earlier case, without carrying out the argument on that case

examples.name: Families of quadrics and Severi--Brauer varieties
examples.note: These are cited as geometric classes where the fibration method can give analogous arithmetic results even when the descent separation into a torus situation is unavailable.
